title: Ruth Slenczynska: The Last Student of Rachmaninoff Dies at 101
summary: Ruth Slenczynska, a virtuoso pianist and the last surviving pupil of Sergei Rachmaninoff, died at age 101. Born in California in 1925 to Polish parents, she was a child prodigy who gave her first recital at four and debuted with a full orchestra in Paris at seven. Known for her impeccable technique and musical insight, she performed for five U.S. presidents, including a four-hand Mozart duet with Harry Truman at the White House. Her career spanned nine decades, and she released her final album in 2022. Slenczynska's father, a violinist and teacher, recognized her potential at birth and moved the family to Europe for the best training. She met Rachmaninoff in 1934 after substituting for him in a concert. She continued teaching into her later years, and her former pupil Shelly Moorman-Stahlman reported that Slenczynska was mentally clear and played piano during recent visits. After a series of falls, she died peacefully in an assisted living facility in California, surrounded by friends. Her legacy as one of the greatest child prodigies since Mozart and her contributions to music are remembered.

background_read: Ruth Slenczynska was a renowned pianist whose career spanned over 90 years. She was born in 1925 and showed extraordinary talent early on. Her father, a violinist and teacher, dedicated himself to her musical education, moving the family to Europe for access to top teachers. She studied with Sergei Rachmaninoff, one of the greatest composers of the 20th century. Slenczynska performed for five U.S. presidents, demonstrating her status as a cultural icon. Despite her fame, she remained dedicated to teaching, passing on her knowledge to new generations. Her death at 101 marks the end of an era, but her recordings and legacy continue to inspire.
Article_Structure: Main Points: Ruth Slenczynska, last pupil of Rachmaninoff, died at 101; she was a child prodigy, performed for five presidents, and had a nine-decade career. Purpose: To report her death and celebrate her life and achievements. Evidence Evaluation: The article uses quotes from her former pupil and historical sources like the New York Times, providing credible firsthand accounts. Author Credibility: BBC is a reputable news source. Methodology: The article gathers information from statements and archival material, presenting a factual obituary.

perspectives.description: Ruth was celebrated as one of the greatest child prodigies since Mozart, with exceptional talent from a very young age.
Even in her final years, she continued to teach and inspire students, showing her commitment to music education.
